rationale_for_baseline_tier: The most likely diagnosis is benign globus pharyngeus with acute anxiety over a stable euthyroid goitre, and the reassuring patient-obtainable features (full-sentence speech, no audible stridor, preserved swallowing, no cyanosis) argue strongly against an airway emergency — so a 000 ambulance (T5) or ED (T4) is over-triage for the current picture, which is exactly the unnecessary dispatch the source encounter avoided. However, telehealth cannot palpate the trachea, assess the goitre, or auscultate the chest to structurally exclude compromise the way the in-person clinician did, and the patient has a known thyroid mass and is acutely distressed; pure self-care with no review (T0/T1) would under-serve that residual uncertainty. Prompt GP review within a day or two (T2) with a firm airway safety net is the honest, conservative telehealth tier. Routine GP within a week (T1) is an acceptable pass if the patient settles and all reassuring features hold.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: High-pitched whistling or noisy sound on breathing in (stridor)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately — call 000 the moment this appears
escalation_edges.rationale: New stridor indicates true upper-airway narrowing and is a time-critical emergency requiring an ambulance.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you start to hear a high-pitched whistling or noisy sound when you breathe in, call 000 for an ambulance straight away — do not wait.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Lips or face turning blue or grey (cyanosis)
escalation_edges.rationale: Cyanosis signals critical hypoxia and possible airway compromise — an immediate emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your lips or face start to turn blue, call 000 immediately.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Sudden severe hoarseness or loss of voice
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Go to the nearest Emergency Department without delay
escalation_edges.rationale: A sudden voice change can indicate an expanding or haemorrhagic thyroid lesion affecting the larynx and needs urgent assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your voice suddenly becomes very hoarse or you lose it, go straight to an Emergency Department.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Becoming completely unable to swallow liquids, or drooling saliva
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Go to the Emergency Department immediately
escalation_edges.rationale: True inability to swallow liquids with a neck mass suggests obstructing pathology requiring urgent evaluation.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you become completely unable to swallow liquids, or start drooling because you cannot swallow, go to an Emergency Department straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Rapidly enlarging, acutely painful or tender neck swelling
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Go to the Emergency Department immediately
escalation_edges.rationale: Rapid enlargement or new pain in the goitre can indicate haemorrhage into the gland with airway threat.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the swelling in your neck rapidly gets bigger or suddenly becomes painful, go to an Emergency Department right away.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Known thyroid goitre combined with high health anxiety
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Reassurance must be paired with a concrete, specific safety net and a definite follow-up rather than a bare 'it's just anxiety' — otherwise the anxiety escalates and the patient re-presents to ED. The known mass also means telehealth cannot fully exclude a structural cause, justifying prompt GP review.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: At home alone this evening while acutely distressed
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Confirm the patient can summon help quickly if a red flag develops, and offer telehealth re-contact if she remains distressed but stable.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Your symptoms are most likely a throat-tightness (globus) sensation driven by anxiety over your known, stable thyroid lump, and the fact that you can speak in full sentences, hear no whistling when you breathe in, and can swallow water are all reassuring that your airway is open. Stop pressing on your neck, breathe slowly, and take small sips of cool water. Arrange to see your GP within the next day or two for the anxiety and a routine thyroid check. This chat cannot examine your neck, so watch closely for the warning signs below.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Book a GP appointment within 1–2 days; call your GP or a nurse triage line sooner if you remain distressed.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If it is after hours and you cannot settle, contact an after-hours GP or nurse triage line; if any airway warning sign appears, call 000 or go to the nearest Emergency Department immediately.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Note the acute globus/anxiety episode over a known euthyroid Hashimoto's goitre, the reassuring airway features elicited by chat, and that a telehealth examination was not possible — GP to examine the neck and arrange routine thyroid ultrasound and TFT confirmation.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing the presentation as 'just anxiety' and closing the encounter with no airway safety net and no follow-up, in a patient with a known thyroid mass whom telehealth cannot examine.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An evolving goitre haemorrhage or airway compromise goes unrecognised because the patient was given no warning signs to act on.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Dispatching a 000 ambulance or directing to ED for a structurally patent airway with strong reassuring features, driven by the patient's alarming 'choking' language — unnecessary emergency-service use that reinforces health anxiety, the exact lights-and-sirens dispatch the source case was designed to avoid.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Telehealth cannot palpate the trachea/goitre or auscultate the chest to structurally exclude an airway-threatening cause, so the benign globus/anxiety diagnosis remains presumptive.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Weight the reassuring patient-obtainable features, de-escalate without dispatching an ambulance, and pair reassurance with a specific airway safety net and prompt GP review.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either claiming false certainty that the airway is definitively safe, or over-reacting with a reflexive 000/ED dispatch for a reassuring presentation.
